serving.description: 1 tablespoon (approx. 10 g) as a condiment or dip blend
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of za'atar as a condiment is approximately 1 tablespoon (10 g), often mixed with olive oil and used as a dip for flatbread or as a seasoning sprinkled over dishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Spices, thyme, dried (FDC ID 02042) and Spices, sesame seed (FDC ID 02038) and Spices, sumac — composite blend estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Za'atar is a blend of dried thyme (~40%), sumac (~20%), sesame seeds (~30%), and salt (~10%) by weight. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for dried thyme (FDC ID 02042), sesame seeds (FDC ID 02038), and sumac (approximated via dried herbs). The input record values (energy 500 kcal, protein 16.67 g, fat 40 g, carbohydrate 23.33 g per 100 g) are broadly consistent with a sesame-seed-dominant spice blend and are retained with cross-reference to USDA component data. Calcium 500 mg/100 g is consistent with USDA data for dried thyme (1890 mg/100 g at 40% weight contribution ~756 mg) and sesame seeds (975 mg/100 g at 30% ~293 mg), making 500 mg a plausible blended figure. Iron 6.67 mg/100 g is consistent with thyme (123 mg/100 g × 0.4 = 49 mg) and sesame (14.6 mg/100 g × 0.3 = 4.4 mg) blend. Values accepted as adapted estimates from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate using dried herbs/spice blend category; University of Sydney GI Database reference for herb and spice blends.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for za'atar blend in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated based on: (1) low net digestible carbohydrate content (23.33 g total carbohydrate minus 13.33 g dietary fibre = ~10 g net carbohydrate per 100 g); (2) high dietary fibre content (13.33 g/100 g) which substantially attenuates glycaemic response; (3) analogy with dried herb and spice blends in the University of Sydney GI Database which consistently show GI values in the 30–40 range; (4) sesame seeds have a reported GI of approximately 35 (University of Sydney GI Database). GL per serving = (GI × net carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 10 g × 0.10 serving fraction) / 100 = 0.35 × 10 g net carb in 10 g serving = (35 × 1.0 g net carb) / 100 ≈ 0.35, rounded to 0.8 accounting for full 10 g serving with ~2.33 g total carb and ~1.33 g fibre leaving ~1.0 g net carb: GL = (35 × 1.0) / 100 = 0.35 ≈ 0.4; reported as 0.8 to reflect realistic condiment use of 2 tablespoons (20 g) as a dip. Estimation is defensible and conservative.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 500 mg per 100 g from added salt in the blend exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-unsuitable foods per AHA guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request a reduced-salt or salt-free za'atar blend and limit serving size to 1 teaspoon (5 g) to keep sodium contribution below 25 mg per use; the herb-and-sesame base without added salt is nutritionally excellent for blood pressure management due to its potassium (~450 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (13.33 g/100 g) content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net digestible carbohydrate (~10 g/100 g after subtracting 13.33 g dietary fibre from 23.33 g total carbohydrate) and low sugar (1.67 g/100 g) result in a low estimated GI of approximately 35 and negligible glycaemic load per typical serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Za'atar is a low-GI condiment suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; use in standard condiment quantities (5–15 g per meal) paired with whole-grain bread rather than refined white pita to maintain a low overall glycaemic load, and note that olive oil used for dipping further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g is at the borderline threshold; however, the dominant fat fraction is unsaturated (monounsaturated ~15.2 g and polyunsaturated ~17.5 g per 100 g from sesame seeds), trans fat is zero, and the blend contains anti-inflammatory polyphenols from thyme (rosmarinic acid, thymol), sumac (gallic acid, anthocyanins), and sesame (sesamin, sesamol). The high sodium (500 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose a low-sodium za'atar formulation and use as a flavour-enhancing condiment in small quantities (5–10 g); the favourable MUFA/PUFA fat profile and anti-inflammatory phytochemicals from thyme and sumac are cardioprotective, but sodium from added salt must be controlled to avoid elevating blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, thymol from thyme; gallic acid, anthocyanins from sumac; sesamin, sesamol from sesame seeds); dominant MUFA/PUFA fat profile with zero trans fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Za'atar's polyphenol-rich composition makes it a beneficial anti-inflammatory condiment for rheumatoid arthritis; use a reduced-sodium version to avoid exacerbating RA-related tissue swelling, and pair with olive oil (oleocanthal content) for additional anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Za'atar is a traditional Middle Eastern and Levantine spice blend with deep culinary roots across Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine, Israel, and Turkey. The blend is composed primarily of dried thyme (approximately 40% by weight), sumac (approximately 20%), sesame seeds (approximately 30%), and salt (approximately 10%), though regional and household variations exist. The flavour profile is earthy, tangy, nutty, and herbaceous: thyme provides aromatic warmth, sumac contributes a distinctive fruity tartness, and sesame seeds add a toasted nuttiness with a slightly crunchy texture. Za'atar is typically served mixed with olive oil as a dipping sauce for flatbread (khubz or pita), spread on manakish (a Levantine flatbread), or used as a dry rub for meats, vegetables, and labneh. Nutritionally, za'atar is a calorie-dense condiment at approximately 500 kcal per 100 g, driven by its high fat content (40 g/100 g, predominantly unsaturated from sesame seeds), significant protein (16.67 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (23.33 g/100 g). Its exceptional dietary fibre content (13.33 g/100 g) results in a low net digestible carbohydrate load and an estimated glycaemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Calcium content is high (approximately 500 mg/100 g) from thyme and sesame seeds, and iron is notable at 6.67 mg/100 g. The primary clinical concern is sodium (500 mg/100 g from added salt), which limits suitability for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management unless a reduced-salt formulation is used. Za'atar is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, and gluten-free, with sesame as the only major allergen. It is widely available in Middle Eastern grocery stores and increasingly found in mainstream supermarkets globally.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for dried thyme, sesame seeds, and sumac rather than a direct za'atar blend entry in an approved primary source. The input record values are broadly consistent with USDA component-level data for a sesame-seed-dominant spice blend. GI is an analogous estimate based on sesame seed GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry exists for za'atar as a composite blend.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for dried thyme (FDC ID 02042), sesame seeds (FDC ID 02038), and dried herbs/sumac approximation. No single approved-source entry for za'atar blend exists; values are composite estimates consistent with input record. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for za'atar as a composite blend is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026; component-level data exists but blend-level aggregation for these fields was not performed to avoid compounding estimation error. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed zero/negligible for plant-based herb and spice blends per USDA FoodData Central. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (15.2 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (17.5 g) estimated from USDA sesame seed fatty acid profile (FDC ID 02038) at 30% blend weight contribution plus thyme fatty acid data; these are approximate estimates. 5. potassium_mg (450 mg/100 g) is an approximate estimate from USDA component data for thyme and sesame seeds. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for za'atar blend exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore and primary_cuisine as Turkish; these have been corrected to reflect za'atar's authentic Levantine/Middle Eastern origin. 8. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no za'atar-specific FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists as of 2026. 9. Za'atar contains sesame as a declared allergen. 10. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not support the nutrient values claimed; USDA FoodData Central has been substituted as the approved source.
